critic_raw:
The design explicitly acknowledges in Section 2 that the stratified random split 'may unintentionally mix future and past data in training and test sets'—and then proceeds with it anyway. This is a critical admission that the design knowingly introduces temporal leakage. For a 30-day readmission outcome in a hospital system, temporal ordering matters enormously: clinical practice patterns change over time (new drugs, updated discharge protocols, hospital capacity changes), and a model trained on 2022 data evaluated on 2019 data is not a valid generalization test. The correct approach is temporal holdout.

The inclusion criterion—all inpatient discharges with complete EHR records AND linked genomic sequencing results—introduces a severe selection bias that is not acknowledged. Genomic sequencing in a hospital system is not standard of care; it is ordered for specific patients based on clinical suspicion, research protocol enrollment, or cancer diagnosis. The population with both complete EHR and genomic data is therefore a highly selected, non-representative subset of the general inpatient population. The design's readmission prediction model will be evaluated on this selected population, but any deployment claim would require it to perform on the general inpatient population—most of whom will not have genomic data.

The multi-modal model's advantage over the baseline is artificially inflated by data scope differences: the baseline uses only demographic and clinical EHR features, while the multi-modal model also uses clinical notes (BERT embeddings) and genomic features. This is not a fair feature-set comparison. The correct ablation would hold modality constant and vary architecture.

Section 6 describes a suspicious test-set protocol: 'a final check is run on the test set to verify that the selected configuration maintains its advantage before committing to full training.' This is soft test-set peeking—a form of conditional test access that biases reported performance upward.
defender_raw:
The multi-modal deep learning readmission prediction design makes a genuine scientific contribution by integrating three heterogeneous data sources—demographic/administrative features, clinical EHR features, and genomic data—that are rarely combined in readmission prediction. The modular branch architecture is appropriate: each data type has a different statistical structure (tabular, sequential text, sparse genomic), and processing each through dedicated sub-networks before fusion allows the model to learn modality-specific representations before integration. This is more principled than concatenating raw features and hoping a single network handles the heterogeneity.

The baseline comparison is designed fairly: logistic regression on demographic and EHR features only (excluding genomic and notes) tests whether the added data modalities justify the added architectural complexity, which is the right scientific question. Including per-site AUC-ROC as a secondary metric is a sophisticated design choice that surfaces institutional bias and tests whether the multi-modal model's advantage is uniform across the healthcare system or concentrated in specific sites with richer data.

Using frozen pre-trained clinical BERT embeddings for discharge notes rather than fine-tuning from scratch is an appropriate choice given the dataset size (10,000+ notes). Fine-tuning a large transformer on ~8,000 training notes risks overfitting to the specific institutional language of the EHR system rather than learning generalizable clinical semantics. Pre-trained embeddings from BioBERT or SciBERT encode broad clinical language patterns validated on millions of clinical documents, providing a stable feature representation. The decision to use AUC-ROC as the primary metric is well-justified for readmission prediction where the operating threshold is clinician-determined based on available intervention capacity.
all_issues_raised: Temporal leakage acknowledged but retained; stratified random split mixes 2019 and 2023 data across train/test despite temporal dependencies
Inclusion requires both complete EHR and genomic data, selecting a non-representative high-acuity subpopulation, invalidating deployment generalization claims
Multi-modal model uses three modalities while baseline uses one; outperformance conflates data richness with architectural benefit
Test-set peek described in Section 6 ('verify advantage before committing to full training') biases reported performance upward
Genomic features (rare variant burden, polygenic risk scores) have high missingness in typical EHR systems; completeness assumption is unrealistic for deployment
